```mermaid
    F4 --> F7[Working Capital and Term Loan]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** Each node labelled `F1`, `S1`, `P1` follows the alphanumeric rule (prefixed with letter, never using reserved words like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
**Define KVIC and state any four of its objectives.**

**Model Answer:**
KVIC, the Khadi and Village Industries Commission, is a statutory body established under the Khadi and Village Industries Commission Act, 1956, under the Ministry of MSME, with its headquarters in Mumbai.

**Four Objectives:**
1. To promote and develop Khadi and other village industries.
2. To create self-employment opportunities in rural and semi-urban areas.
3. To provide financial assistance and training to artisans.
4. To preserve traditional crafts and ensure sustainable rural livelihood.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for any four objectives)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**What is PMEGP? State its objectives.**

**Model Answer:**
PMEGP (Prime Minister's Employment Generation Programme) is a flagship credit-linked subsidy scheme launched in August 2008, implemented by KVIC for generating employment through micro-enterprises.

**Objectives:**
1. Generate continuous and sustainable employment in rural and urban areas.
2. Encourage unemployed youth to set up micro-enterprises.
3. Prevent rural-to-urban migration by creating local employment.

*(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain in detail the organizational structure, objectives, and functions of KVIC. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the Prime Minister's Employment Generation Programme (PMEGP) with reference to eligibility, funding pattern, and subsidy calculation. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Organizational Structure of KVIC:**
KVIC operates at three tiers:
- **Apex Level:** Head Office at Mumbai with a Chairman and members.
- **State Level:** 36 State Khadi & Village Industries Boards (KVIBs).
- **District Level:** District Industries Centres (DICs) and Khadi institutions.

**Objectives (as per Section 4 of KVIC Act, 1956):**
1. Plan, promote, and organize Khadi and village industries.
2. Train artisans and improve their skill levels.
3. Provide financial and technical support.
4. Establish marketing linkages.

**Functions:**
1. Implementation of PMEGP, SFURTI, REGP, etc.
2. Disbursement of subsidy through banks.
3. Quality control and branding of Khadi products.
4. Coordination with State KVIBs and NGOs.

*(7 marks: 3 marks structure + 2 marks objectives + 2 marks functions)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Eligibility for PMEGP:**
- Age $\geq$ 18 years.
- Minimum 8th pass for projects above Rs. 10 lakh in manufacturing.
- Annual family income $\leq$ Rs. 1 lakh (rural) / Rs. 2 lakh (urban).

**Funding Pattern Table:**

| Component | General | Reserved Category |
|:----------|:--------|:------------------|
| Own Contribution | 10% | 5% |
| Subsidy (Rural) | 15% | 25% |
| Bank Loan | Balance | Balance |

**Subsidy Calculation Example:**
For a general category male in rural area with project cost Rs. 10 lakh:

$$\text{Own Contribution} = 0.10 \times 10{,}00{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 1{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{Subsidy} = 0.15 \times 10{,}00{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 1{,}50{,}000$$

$$\text{Bank Loan} = 10{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 7{,}50{,}000$$

*(7 marks: 2 marks eligibility + 3 marks funding table + 2 marks numerical)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**(a)** Explain the SFURTI scheme with its cluster types and funding support. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between REGP and PMEGP. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**SFURTI — Scheme of Fund for Regeneration of Traditional Industries** was launched to organize traditional industries into clusters.

**Cluster Types and Funding:**

| Cluster Type | Artisans | Project Cost | GoI Support |
|:-------------|:---------|:-------------|:------------|
| Heritage | $\leq 500$ | Up to Rs. 8 Crore | 100% |
| Regular | $\leq 1000$ | Up to Rs. 3 Crore | 90% |
| Tiny | $\leq 250$ | Up to Rs. 1.5 Crore | 80% |

**Key Benefits:**
1. Common Facility Centre (CFC) setup.
2. Skill upgradation and design intervention.
3. Brand building and market access.

*(7 marks: 2 marks introduction + 3 marks table + 2 marks benefits)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

| Parameter | REGP | PMEGP |
|:----------|:-----|:------|
| Year | 1995 | 2008 |
| Area | Rural only | Rural + Urban |
| Project Ceiling | Rs. 25 lakh | Rs. 25 lakh (MFG), Rs. 10 lakh (Service) |
| Subsidy (Reserved) | Up to 30% | Up to 25% |
| Subsidy (General) | 15-25% | 15% |
| Status | Merged into PMEGP | Active |

**Key Difference:** REGP was restricted to rural areas only; PMEGP is a merged, expanded scheme covering rural and urban areas with uniform parameters across the country.

*(7 marks: 5 marks table + 2 marks concluding difference)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Don't write PMRY** — it was discontinued in 2008. Always cite PMEGP.
> 2. **Don't confuse KVIC with NABARD or SIDBI** — KVIC is for Khadi/Village; SIDBI is for MSME finance.
> 3. **Subsidy is BACK-ENDED** — students lose 1 mark by writing it is upfront.
> 4. **Project cost ceiling for service sector is Rs. 10 lakh**, not Rs. 25 lakh.
> 5. **Reserved category in Rural = 25% subsidy**, not 15%. Read the question carefully.
> 6. **Sanctioning authority for PMEGP is the BANK**, not KVIC. KVIC only releases subsidy.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize These 10 Points:**
>
> - ✅ KVIC was established in **1956 (Act)** and operational from **1957**.
> - ✅ Headquarters: **Mumbai**, Ministry: **MSME**.
> - ✅ **PMEGP** = flagship scheme for employment generation (since 2008).
> - ✅ Project ceiling: **Rs. 25 lakh (MFG)**, **Rs. 10 lakh (Service)**.
> - ✅ Subsidy rate: **15% (General)**, **25% (Reserved Rural)**.
> - ✅ Own contribution: **10% (General)**, **5% (Reserved)**.
> - ✅ **SFURTI** focuses on **cluster development** for traditional industries.
> - ✅ **MDA** = Market Development Assistance for Khadi.
> - ✅ **ISEC** = Interest Subsidy Eligibility Certificate (Khadi only).
> - ✅ **MGIRI** = Research & Training institute (Wardha, Maharashtra).

**Key Formulas for Quick Reference:**

$$\text{Subsidy} = \text{Rate} \times \text{Project Cost}$$

$$\text{Bank Loan} = \text{Project Cost} - \text{Own Contribution} - \text{Subsidy}$$

**Mnemonic for PMEGP Limits:** **"25 — 10 — 15 — 25"**
*(25 lakh MFG \vert 10 lakh Service \vert 15% General Subsidy \vert 25% Reserved Subsidy)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Entrepreneurial Ideation and Decision Process; Incubation Procedures and Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT743 - ENTREPRENEURSHIP
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** The Entrepreneurial Ideation and Decision Process; Incubation Procedures and Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 The Entrepreneurial Ideation Process — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Entrepreneurial Ideation** is the structured, iterative cognitive and creative procedure through which an entrepreneur identifies problems, scans the environment for opportunities, generates a high volume of innovative ideas, filters them using feasibility heuristics, and finally crystallizes a single, viable **Business Concept** that can be translated into a product or service offering.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned), the ideation process is treated as the **front-end funnel** of the entrepreneurial value chain — it precedes feasibility analysis, business modelling, and resource mobilization. It is governed by **divergent** (broad) and **convergent** (narrowing) thinking modes.

### 1.2 The Decision Process — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Entrepreneurial Decision Process** is the rational evaluation framework applied to shortlisted ideas. It involves setting **decision criteria** (market attractiveness, technical feasibility, financial viability, risk profile, and personal fit), assigning weights, scoring alternatives, and committing capital, time, and effort to a chosen course of action — typically culminating in a **Go / No-Go** decision.

## 1.3 Incubation Procedures and Processes — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Business Incubation** is a formal, time-bound support programme (usually **6 months to 3 years**) run by an incubator (academic, corporate, government, or private) that provides early-stage startups with a bundled package of **workspace, mentorship, funding access, networking, legal/accounting support, and prototyping infrastructure** to accelerate their journey from concept to commercialization.

---

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen-to-Restaurant" Model

To make these three abstract constructs intuitive for a first-time reader, imagine the entire entrepreneurial journey as opening a restaurant:

| Stage in Analogy | Real-World Equivalent | Output |
|---|---|---|
| Tasting random ingredients & thinking of new dishes | **Ideation Process** | A pool of dish ideas |
| Tasting, family voting, cost analysis of the menu | **Decision Process** | A finalized 10-item menu |
| Renting a test kitchen, hiring a head chef, perfecting recipes | **Incubation Process** | A signature menu ready for launch |

### Why this analogy works
- An **idea** (dish concept) is cheap to imagine but expensive to execute.
- A **decision** (menu selection) requires balancing taste, cost, and customer appeal.
- **Incubation** (test kitchen) is the controlled environment where raw ideas are refined into market-ready products without the high risk of a public launch.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Heuristic:** Whenever you write a 14-mark answer on this topic, anchor your introduction to the *funnel metaphor* — wide at ideation, narrowing at decision, focused at incubation. Examiners reward structured framing.

---

## 1.5 Standard Metrics Used in This Module

The following standardized metrics and indicators must be memorized for the KTU ESE (End Semester Examination):

- **TAM (Total Addressable Market)** — the total revenue opportunity if 100% market share is captured.
- **SAM (Serviceable Available Market)** — the segment of TAM targeted by your product.
- **SOM (Serviceable Obtainable Market)** — the realistic share you can capture in the short term.
- **IRR (Internal Rate of Return)** — discount rate that makes NPV = 0.
- **NPV (Net Present Value)** — present value of future cash flows minus initial investment.
- **MVP (Minimum Viable Product)** — smallest version of the product that delivers core value.
- **PMF (Product-Market Fit)** — the degree to which a product satisfies strong market demand.
- **TAT (Turn-Around Time)** for incubators — typically **18 to 36 months**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Entrepreneurial Ideation Process — Deconstructed

The ideation process is **not random brainstorming**. It is a five-stage disciplined funnel:

### Stage 1 — Problem Identification
- Entrepreneurs observe **pain points** in the environment.
- Tools: **Empathy mapping, Customer interviews, Field observation, Job-to-be-Done (JTBD) framework**.
- Output: A clearly articulated **Problem Statement**.

### Stage 2 — Opportunity Scanning
- The problem is mapped against **trends, gaps, and un-met needs**.
- Tools: **PESTLE analysis, Trend mapping, White-space analysis, Patent search**.

### Stage 3 — Idea Generation (Divergent Phase)
- High-volume, low-judgment generation of ideas.
- Tools: **Brainstorming, SCAMPER, Mind-mapping, TRIZ, Six Thinking Hats, Analogical thinking**.
- Output: 50–200 raw ideas.

### Stage 4 — Idea Screening (Convergent Phase)
- Filtering using **basic feasibility heuristics** (cost, time, technical know-how, legal issues).
- Tools: **Quick-win matrix, Kill-criteria checklist, Pugh Matrix**.

### Stage 5 — Concept Development & Testing
- The surviving idea is converted into a **Business Concept** with value proposition, target customer, and rough revenue logic.
- Tested with a small sample of potential customers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The transition from Stage 3 to Stage 4 is the **divergent-to-convergent hinge**. Examiners consistently test this transition.

---

## 2.2 The Entrepreneurial Decision Process — Deconstructed

Once an idea survives the funnel, a rigorous decision is made through the following framework:

### Step 1 — Define Decision Criteria
- **Market attractiveness** (size, growth, competition)
- **Technical feasibility** (technology readiness, IP availability)
- **Financial viability** (revenue model, margins, breakeven)
- **Personal fit** (skills, passion, risk tolerance)
- **Strategic alignment** (timing, networking)

### Step 2 — Weight the Criteria
Assign weights $w_i$ such that $\sum w_i = 1$.

### Step 3 — Score Each Alternative Idea
Each idea is scored on a scale (typically **1 to 10**) against each criterion.

### Step 4 — Compute Weighted Score
$$
S_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_{ij}
$$
Where $S_j$ is the composite score of idea $j$, $w_i$ is the weight of criterion $i$, and $s_{ij}$ is the score of idea $j$ against criterion $i$.

### Step 5 — Risk-Adjusted Go / No-Go
Apply **Expected Monetary Value (EMV)** to top scorers:
$$
EMV = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot v_i
$$
Where $p_i$ is the probability of outcome $i$, and $v_i$ is the value of outcome $i$.

### Step 6 — Commitment
Convert the decision into a **Business Plan** with milestones.

---

## 2.3 Business Incubation Procedures and Processes — Deconstructed

Incubation is the **operational bridge** between idea-validation and market-launch. It is structured into **four sequential phases**:

### Phase 1 — Pre-Incubation (0–3 months)
- **Goal:** Validate idea at very low cost.
- Activities: Customer discovery, MVP sketch, basic financial model, IP filing.
- Support: Mentorship hours, co-working access, seed grants up to ₹5–10 lakhs.

### Phase 2 — Incubation (3–18 months)
- **Goal:** Build MVP, achieve first paying customer.
- Activities: Product development, pilot deployment, team formation, traction metrics.
- Support: Dedicated desk, lab access (TBI / STEP / TISC), seed funding up to ₹25 lakhs.

### Phase 3 — Acceleration (18–36 months)
- **Goal:** Achieve PMF and scale.
- Activities: Series A fund-raising, market expansion, channel partnerships.
- Support: Investor connects (via networks like NASSCOM 10K, TiE), advanced mentorship.

### Phase 4 — Graduation (Graduation Day)
- **Goal:** Stand-alone, investor-ready entity.
- Output: Revenue-positive or funded startup that "graduates" from incubator support.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Framework Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula, framework, and threshold** you must remember for the KTU ESE on this topic. Memorize the formulas and the **boundary conditions**.

### Table 2.A — Decision-Scoring Framework

| Component | Symbol | Formula / Rule | Boundary / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Composite Score of Idea $j$ | $S_j$ | $S_j = \sum w_i \cdot s_{ij}$ | $0 \leq S_j \leq 10$ |
| Sum of Weights | $\sum w_i$ | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1$ | Must equal **1** |
| Expected Monetary Value | $EMV$ | $EMV = \sum p_i \cdot v_i$ | $0 \leq p_i \leq 1$ |
| Net Present Value | $NPV$ | $NPV = \sum \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - I_0$ | Accept if $NPV \geq 0$ |
| Internal Rate of Return | $IRR$ | $\sum \frac{CF_t}{(1+IRR)^t} - I_0 = 0$ | Accept if $IRR \geq r$ |
| Payback Period | $PP$ | $PP = \dfrac{I_0}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}}$ | Shorter is better |

### Table 2.B — Incubation Stage Cheat Sheet

| Phase | Duration | Core Deliverable | Funding Range (India) | Exit Criterion |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pre-Incubation | 0–3 months | Validated problem-solution fit | ₹0–10 Lakhs | MVP sketch + 10 customer interviews |
| Incubation | 3–18 months | MVP + First revenue | ₹10–25 Lakhs | First paying customer |
| Acceleration | 18–36 months | PMF + Scaling | ₹25 Lakhs – 5 Cr | ₹1 Cr ARR or Series A |
| Graduation | Day of demo | Investor-ready entity | Self-funded / VC | Sustained revenue or valuation ≥ ₹10 Cr |

### Table 2.C — Ideation Technique Comparison

| Technique | Mode | Best For | Output Volume |
|---|---|---|---|
| Brainstorming | Divergent | Group idea generation | High (50–100) |
| SCAMPER | Divergent | Product innovation | Medium (20–40) |
| Mind Mapping | Divergent | Visual thinkers | Medium |
| Design Thinking | Iterative | Problem framing | Few but deep |
| TRIZ | Systematic | Engineering problems | Few but high-quality |
| Six Thinking Hats | Structured | Group decision quality | Medium |

> [!TIP]
> **Avoid the pipe symbol in tables.** In your answer script, use either a hyphen or describe the absolute value using words to prevent markdown table parsing errors during exam evaluation.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering Entrepreneurship

For a KTU B.Tech student, this framework is **directly applicable**:

- A **final-year engineering project** is essentially an **ideation output**.
- The **decision process** helps select between multiple project ideas using weighted scoring.
- The **incubation framework** allows students to approach the **KTU-recommended TBI (Technology Business Incubator)** in their own campus, or apply to **Kerala Startup Mission (KSUM)** programs for funding up to ₹15 lakhs under the **Student Entrepreneurship Programme (SEP)**.
- **Real utility:** A graduate who masters this funnel can launch a **deep-tech startup** in domains like IoT, AgriTech, FinTech, or MedTech — sectors actively supported by **MeitY TIDE 2.0, DST NIDHI, and BIRAC** schemes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Weighted Decision Matrix (Exhaustive Walkthrough)

Suppose an engineering student, **Ananya**, has three project ideas and must decide which one to take to incubation. She uses a **weighted decision matrix**.

### Step 1 — Define Decision Criteria
She chooses five criteria:
1. Market Size ($C_1$)
2. Technical Feasibility ($C_2$)
3. Personal Passion ($C_3$)
4. Capital Required ($C_4$, lower is better)
5. Social Impact ($C_5$)

### Step 2 — Assign Weights
Based on her priorities, she assigns weights:

$$
w_1 = 0.30, \quad w_2 = 0.25, \quad w_3 = 0.20, \quad w_4 = 0.15, \quad w_5 = 0.10
$$

**Check the sum:**
$$
\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i = 0.30 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.10 = 1.00
$$

This satisfies the **mandatory boundary condition** $\sum w_i = 1$.

### Step 3 — Score Each Idea (1–10 scale)

| Criterion | Weight $w_i$ | Idea A: Smart Irrigation | Idea B: AI Chatbot | Idea C: Solar Tracker |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $C_1$ Market Size | 0.30 | 8 | 9 | 7 |
| $C_2$ Technical Feasibility | 0.25 | 7 | 6 | 9 |
| $C_3$ Personal Passion | 0.20 | 9 | 7 | 8 |
| $C_4$ Capital Required | 0.15 | 6 | 9 | 5 |
| $C_5$ Social Impact | 0.10 | 9 | 6 | 8 |

### Step 4 — Compute Composite Score for Idea A (Smart Irrigation)

$$
S_A = (0.30 \cdot 8) + (0.25 \cdot 7) + (0.20 \cdot 9) + (0.15 \cdot 6) + (0.10 \cdot 9)
$$

$$
S_A = 2.40 + 1.75 + 1.80 + 0.90 + 0.90
$$

$$
S_A = 7.75
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Composite Score for Idea B (AI Chatbot)

$$
S_B = (0.30 \cdot 9) + (0.25 \cdot 6) + (0.20 \cdot 7) + (0.15 \cdot 9) + (0.10 \cdot 6)
$$

$$
S_B = 2.70 + 1.50 + 1.40 + 1.35 + 0.60
$$

$$
S_B = 7.55
$$

### Step 6 — Compute Composite Score for Idea C (Solar Tracker)

$$
S_C = (0.30 \cdot 7) + (0.25 \cdot 9) + (0.20 \cdot 8) + (0.15 \cdot 5) + (0.10 \cdot 8)
$$

$$
S_C = 2.10 + 2.25 + 1.60 + 0.75 + 0.80
$$

$$
S_C = 7.50
$$

### Step 7 — Rank and Decide

$$
S_A = 7.75 \quad > \quad S_B = 7.55 \quad > \quad S_C = 7.50
$$

**Decision:** Ananya pursues **Idea A: Smart Irrigation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight for Students:** Notice how a *qualitative* decision ("which project?") was converted into a *quantitative* decision using $\sum w_i \cdot s_{ij}$. This is the **engineer's mindset** applied to entrepreneurship.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Expected Monetary Value (EMV) for Risk-Adjusted Decision

After shortlisting Idea A, Ananya estimates three scenarios:

| Outcome | Probability $p_i$ | Net Value $v_i$ (in ₹ Lakhs) |
|---|---|---|
| Success | 0.30 | +50 |
| Moderate | 0.50 | +10 |
| Failure | 0.20 | −15 |

### Step 1 — Apply the EMV Formula

$$
EMV = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot v_i
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Values

$$
EMV = (0.30 \cdot 50) + (0.50 \cdot 10) + (0.20 \cdot (-15))
$$

$$
EMV = 15 + 5 + (-3)
$$

$$
EMV = 17 \text{ Lakhs}
$$

### Step 3 — Interpret
Since $EMV > 0$ and is positive, the project is **financially justified**. The decision threshold is:
$$
\text{Accept if } EMV > 0
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** Always state the **decision rule** explicitly in your answer — examiners reward the *threshold logic*, not just the calculation.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Ideation to Incubation

The following analytical matrix connects every ideation-output to its corresponding incubation-stage activity:

| Ideation Stage Output | Decision Stage Filter | Incubation Stage Activity | KTU Funding Instrument |
|---|---|---|---|
| Problem Statement | Problem-Solution Fit Test | Customer Discovery Interviews | KSUM Idea Grant (₹2 L) |
| Raw Ideas (50+) | Divergent Brainstorming | Brainstorming Sessions | In-house Seed |
| Shortlisted Ideas (5–10) | Weighted Scoring Matrix | Pugh Matrix Refinement | TBI Pre-Seed |
| Business Concept | Go / No-Go (EMV) | MVP Development | DST NIDHI PRAYAS (₹10 L) |
| Validated Concept | Pilot Approval | Pilot Deployment | MeitY TIDE 2.0 (₹7 L) |
| Revenue-Generating MVP | Graduation Decision | Investor Pitching | Series A / VC Funding |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — NPV Computation for Incubation Investment

Suppose Ananya's Smart-Irrigation startup requires an initial investment of $I_0 = ₹20$ Lakhs and is expected to generate cash inflows of $CF_1 = ₹8$ Lakhs, $CF_2 = ₹12$ Lakhs, $CF_3 = ₹15$ Lakhs at a discount rate $r = 10\%$.

### Step 1 — Discount Each Cash Flow

$$
PV_1 = \frac{8}{(1.10)^1} = \frac{8}{1.10} = 7.27 \text{ Lakhs}
$$

$$
PV_2 = \frac{12}{(1.10)^2} = \frac{12}{1.21} = 9.92 \text{ Lakhs}
$$

$$
PV_3 = \frac{15}{(1.10)^3} = \frac{15}{1.331} = 11.27 \text{ Lakhs}
$$

### Step 2 — Sum the Present Values

$$
\sum PV_t = 7.27 + 9.92 + 11.27 = 28.46 \text{ Lakhs}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute NPV

$$
NPV = \sum \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - I_0
$$

$$
NPV = 28.46 - 20.00 = +8.46 \text{ Lakhs}
$$

### Step 4 — Decision Rule
Since $NPV > 0$, the project **creates value** and should be accepted.

$$
\text{Decision: } NPV = +8.46 \text{ Lakhs} \implies \text{Proceed to Incubation}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Entrepreneurial Ideation Funnel

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[OBSERVATION: Pain Points and Trends] --> nodeB[PROBLEM IDENTIFICATION: Empathy Map and JTBD]
    nodeB --> nodeC[OPPORTUNITY SCANNING: PESTLE and White Space Analysis]
    nodeC --> nodeD[IDEA GENERATION: Brainstorming, SCAMPER, TRIZ]
    nodeD --> nodeE[IDEA SCREENING: Kill Criteria, Pugh Matrix]
    nodeE --> nodeF[CONCEPT TESTING: Customer Interviews, Prototype Feedback]
    nodeF --> nodeG[VALIDATED BUSINESS CONCEPT: Ready for Decision Process]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The funnel widens at the top (broad observation) and narrows down to a single validated concept. Each stage filters and refines the output of the previous stage.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — The Decision Process Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Input: Multiple Ideas from Ideation Funnel] --> stepB[Step 1: Define Decision Criteria]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 2: Assign Weights Sum Equals 1]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 3: Score Each Idea 1 to 10]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 4: Compute Weighted Score Sj]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 5: Compute EMV for Top 3]
    stepF --> stepG{Decision: EMV greater than 0?}
    stepG -->|Yes| stepH[Step 6: Go to Business Plan]
    stepG -->|No| stepI[Step 6: No-Go - Iterate or Pivot]
    stepH --> stepJ[Output: Chosen Idea with Feasibility Proof]
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Business Incubation Lifecycle (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PHASE1[PHASE 1: PRE-INCUBATION 0 to 3 months]
        pa[Idea Validation] --> pb[Customer Discovery]
        pb --> pc[MVP Sketch]
    end
    subgraph PHASE2[PHASE 2: INCUBATION 3 to 18 months]
        pd[Product Development] --> pe[Pilot Launch]
        pe --> pf[First Revenue]
    end
    subgraph PHASE3[PHASE 3: ACCELERATION 18 to 36 months]
        pg[Series A Funding] --> ph[Market Expansion]
        ph --> pi[Channel Partnerships]
    end
    subgraph PHASE4[PHASE 4: GRADUATION]
        pj[Investor Demo Day] --> pk[Independent Scaling]
    end
    PHASE1 --> PHASE2
    PHASE2 --> PHASE3
    PHASE3 --> PHASE4
```

**Key Observation:** Each phase has a **gate-criterion** — failure to meet it results in either phase-extension or exit from the incubator.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Stakeholder Interaction Map in Incubation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    founder[Founder Team] --> tbi[Technology Business Incubator TBI]
    tbi --> mentor1[Domain Mentor]
    tbi --> mentor2[Business Mentor]
    tbi --> mentor3[Technical Mentor]
    tbi --> funding[Funding Bodies: KSUM, DST, MeitY, BIRAC]
    tbi --> investor[Angel Investors and VCs]
    tbi --> legal[Legal and IP Support]
    tbi --> proto[Prototyping Lab Access]
    founder --> customer[Early Adopter Customers]
    customer --> feedback[Feedback Loop]
    feedback --> founder
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Q: Define the Entrepreneurial Ideation Process. Briefly explain any TWO ideation techniques.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** The Entrepreneurial Ideation Process is the systematic, creative procedure of generating, screening, and refining business ideas to identify a viable opportunity. It involves divergent thinking (generating many ideas) and convergent thinking (filtering them).

> **Technique 1 — Brainstorming (0.75 marks):** A group technique where participants freely propose ideas without criticism, aiming for high volume. **Rule:** Defer judgment, build on others' ideas, aim for quantity.

> **Technique 2 — SCAMPER (0.75 marks):** A checklist-based technique that asks questions like *Substitute, Combine, Adapt, Modify, Put to another use, Eliminate, Rearrange* — useful for product innovation.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Q: Differentiate between Pre-Incubation and Incubation phases of a startup.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Pre-Incubation | Incubation |
|---|---|---|
| **Duration** | 0–3 months | 3–18 months |
| **Goal** | Validate idea | Build MVP & get first customer |
| **Funding** | Up to ₹5–10 Lakhs | ₹10–25 Lakhs |
| **Output** | Validated concept | Revenue-generating MVP |
| **Support** | Mentorship, desk space | Lab access, seed funding |

> (1 mark for definition; 2 marks for comparison)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Many students **confuse ideation with brainstorming** — ideation is a *complete funnel*, brainstorming is *just one technique*.
> 2. Students often **skip the decision criteria step** and jump straight to scoring — this loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. For incubation questions, students forget to mention **specific Indian schemes** (KSUM, NIDHI, TIDE 2.0) — examiners reward contextual answers.
> 4. Always **draw a diagram** (funnel or lifecycle) — diagrams carry **2 easy marks**.
> 5. Never write a 14-mark answer as a single paragraph — use **sub-headings and bullets**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**

**Q: Explain in detail the Entrepreneurial Ideation Process and the Decision Process used by an entrepreneur to select a viable idea.**

#### Part (a) — Entrepreneurial Ideation Process (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
The Entrepreneurial Ideation Process is the structured generation and refinement of business ideas through creative and analytical methods.

**2. Stages of Ideation (4 marks):**

- **Stage 1 — Problem Identification:** Use empathy mapping, customer interviews, and field observation to identify unmet needs.
- **Stage 2 — Opportunity Scanning:** Use PESTLE analysis and trend mapping to identify gaps in the market.
- **Stage 3 — Idea Generation (Divergent):** Apply brainstorming, SCAMPER, mind-mapping, and TRIZ to generate 50–200 raw ideas.
- **Stage 4 — Idea Screening (Convergent):** Apply kill-criteria, Pugh Matrix, and quick-win matrix to filter to 5–10 ideas.
- **Stage 5 — Concept Development & Testing:** Convert the surviving idea into a business concept and test with 10–20 potential customers.

**3. Design Thinking as Ideation Framework (2 marks):**
Design thinking follows five iterative stages — *Empathize, Define, Ideate, Prototype, Test*. It is widely used in KTU-promoted student entrepreneurship programs because it places the customer at the center of ideation.

> **Suggested Diagram (Bonus 1 mark):** Draw the Ideation Funnel.

---

#### Part (b) — Entrepreneurial Decision Process (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
The Decision Process is the rational evaluation framework used to select the most viable idea from the shortlisted options.

**2. Steps of Decision Process (4 marks):**

- **Step 1 — Define Decision Criteria:** Market size, technical feasibility, financial viability, personal fit, social impact.
- **Step 2 — Assign Weights:** $w_i$ such that $\sum w_i = 1$.
- **Step 3 — Score Alternatives:** Score each idea on a 1–10 scale.
- **Step 4 — Compute Composite Score:** $S_j = \sum w_i \cdot s_{ij}$.
- **Step 5 — Compute EMV:** $EMV = \sum p_i \cdot v_i$.
- **Step 6 — Go / No-Go Decision:** Apply decision rules like $NPV > 0$ or $EMV > 0$.

**3. Example (2 marks):**
A worked numerical example with at least 3 ideas and 4–5 criteria showing the computation of $S_j$ and the final ranking.

> **Suggested Diagram (Bonus 1 mark):** Draw the Decision Process Flowchart.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**Q: Explain in detail the Business Incubation Procedures and Processes. Discuss the types and roles of incubators in the Indian startup ecosystem.**

#### Part (a) — Incubation Procedures and Processes (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
Business incubation is a structured support programme that helps early-stage startups convert ideas into market-ready products.

**2. Phases of Incubation (4 marks):**

- **Pre-Incubation (0–3 months):** Idea validation, customer discovery, MVP sketch. Funding up to ₹5–10 Lakhs.
- **Incubation (3–18 months):** MVP development, pilot launch, first revenue. Funding ₹10–25 Lakhs.
- **Acceleration (18–36 months):** PMF achievement, Series A funding, market expansion. Funding up to ₹5 Cr.
- **Graduation:** Investor-ready entity, demo day presentation, stand-alone scaling.

**3. Selection Process at Incubators (2 marks):**
- Application with idea summary.
- Screening by expert panel.
- Pitch presentation.
- Due diligence.
- Acceptance and onboarding.

> **Suggested Diagram (Bonus 1 mark):** Draw the 4-Phase Incubation Lifecycle.

---

#### Part (b) — Types and Roles of Incubators (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Types of Incubators (4 marks):**

| Type | Example | Focus |
|---|---|---|
| Academic / TBI | CITTIC CUSAT, TBI-IITM | Student tech startups |
| Government | KSUM, STEP, TISC | Sector-specific innovation |
| Corporate | Microsoft for Startups, T-Hub | Strategic fit with parent firm |
| Private / VC-backed | Y Combinator, 500 Startups | Fast scaling |
| Sector-specific | BIRAC (BioTech), MeitY TIDE 2.0 (Electronics) | Deep-tech / domain-specific |

**2. Roles of Incubators (2 marks):**

- **Workspace:** Co-working desks, lab access.
- **Mentorship:** Domain, business, and technical mentors.
- **Funding:** Seed capital and investor connects.
- **Services:** Legal, accounting, IP filing.
- **Network:** Alumni founders, industry partners, government schemes.

**3. Indian Context (1 mark):**
Mention **DST-NIDHI**, **BIRAC**, **KSUM**, **MeitY TIDE 2.0** as key government schemes that fund incubators.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Module 2**

### Ideation Process
- Ideation is a **funnel**, not a single brainstorming session.
- Five stages: **Observation → Problem ID → Opportunity Scan → Idea Generation → Screening → Concept Test**.
- Divergent phase (volume) → Convergent phase (filter).
- Key techniques: **Brainstorming, SCAMPER, Mind-mapping, TRIZ, Design Thinking**.
- Always define a **Problem Statement** before generating ideas.

### Decision Process
- Six steps: **Criteria → Weights → Scores → Weighted Score → EMV → Go/No-Go**.
- **Boundary Condition:** $\sum w_i = 1$ (mandatory).
- Decision rule: Accept if $NPV > 0$ or $EMV > 0$.
- Tools: **Weighted Scoring Matrix, EMV Analysis, NPV, Pugh Matrix**.
- Composite score formula: $S_j = \sum w_i \cdot s_{ij}$.

### Incubation Procedures
- Four phases: **Pre-Incubation → Incubation → Acceleration → Graduation**.
- Total duration: **0–36 months**.
- Support: **Workspace, Mentorship, Funding, Legal, Lab, Network**.
- Key Indian schemes: **KSUM, DST-NIDHI, BIRAC, MeitY TIDE 2.0**.
- Always mention **gate-criteria** between phases.

### Examination Strategy
- Draw at least **one diagram** in every 14-mark answer.
- Use **Indian context** (KSUM, NIDHI) — examiners reward it.
- Always state the **decision rule / threshold** explicitly.
- Use **sub-headings** — avoid monolithic paragraphs.
- Memorize **one numerical example** of the weighted decision matrix.

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- Confusing ideation with brainstorming.
- Forgetting the $\sum w_i = 1$ condition.
- Skipping the divergent-to-convergent transition explanation.
- Writing incubation as a single phase instead of four.
- Omitting the **Go/No-Go decision rule**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Business Plan Preparation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT743 - ENTREPRENEURSHIP
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Business Plan Preparation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Business Plan (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A **Business Plan** is a written document prepared by an entrepreneur that comprehensively describes a proposed business venture's **vision, mission, objectives, strategies, market, operations, management team, and financial projections**. It serves as a **blueprint** for the startup phase and a **strategic communication tool** for attracting investors, securing bank loans, and guiding operational decision-making over a defined planning horizon (typically **3 to 5 years**).

The term was popularized by **William A. Sahlman** (Harvard Business School, 1997) who emphasized that a business plan's purpose is not merely to raise capital but to **test the viability of a business idea** through rigorous analytical writing.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Architectural Blueprint

Imagine you want to construct a house in Kerala for your family.

You would never begin laying bricks without a **detailed architectural blueprint** that specifies:
- The total plot area and number of rooms
- The plumbing, electrical, and structural layout
- The estimated construction cost
- The timeline for completion

A **Business Plan** serves exactly the same function for a startup:
- The **plot** is your **target market**
- The **rooms** are your **products/services**
- The **plumbing/electrical** is your **operational plan**
- The **construction cost** is your **financial plan**
- The **completion timeline** is your **5-year forecast**

Without this blueprint, the entrepreneur is essentially building blindfolded — and the probability of structural collapse (business failure) is **extremely high**.

## 1.3 Primary Functions of a Business Plan

> [!NOTE]
> **The 4 Strategic Functions (KTU Board-Exam Framework):**

| # | Function | Explanation |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Strategic Planning Tool** | Forces the entrepreneur to think systematically about every business dimension |
| 2 | **Fundraising Document** | Used to convince banks, angel investors, VCs, and government schemes |
| 3 | **Operational Roadmap** | Guides daily, monthly, and yearly operations |
| 4 | **Performance Benchmark** | Provides measurable KPIs against which actual performance is compared |

## 1.4 Who Reads a Business Plan?

The audience determines the **tone, depth, and emphasis** of the document:
- **Banks & Financial Institutions** → Focus on repayment capacity, collateral, cash flow
- **Angel Investors & VCs** → Focus on scalability, exit strategy, ROI
- **Government Agencies** (KSUM, DIC, Startup India) → Focus on innovation, employment generation
- **Internal Management** → Focus on operational milestones and budgets

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 The KTU-Certified Standard Structure of a Business Plan

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **10 Mandatory Sections (Standard SBA / KTU Framework):**

### Section A — The Narrative Sections
1. **Executive Summary**
2. **Company Description**
3. **Products / Services**
4. **Market Analysis**
5. **Marketing & Sales Strategy**
6. **Operations Plan**
7. **Management & Organization**

### Section B — The Financial Sections
8. **Financial Plan**
9. **Funding Request**
10. **Appendices**

---

## 2.2 Deep Dive into Each Component

### 2.2.1 Executive Summary
- Written **LAST** but placed **FIRST** in the document
- Length: **1–2 pages maximum**
- Contains: Business name, mission statement, product/service summary, target market, competitive advantage, financial highlights, funding requirement
- This is the **most-read section** — investors decide within **5 minutes** whether to read further

### 2.2.2 Company Description
- Legal structure: **Sole Proprietorship / Partnership / LLP / Private Limited Company**
- Vision, Mission, and Core Values
- Brief history (if existing) or background of founders
- Location, business premises, and nature of operations

### 2.2.3 Products / Services
- Detailed description of what is being sold
- **Unique Selling Proposition (USP)** — the differentiator
- Stage of development (concept / prototype / market-ready)
- Intellectual Property status (patents, trademarks)

### 2.2.4 Market Analysis
> [!NOTE]
> **The 4 Pillars of Market Analysis:**
> 1. **Industry Analysis** — Size, growth rate, trends, life-cycle stage
> 2. **Target Market Segmentation** — Demographics, psychographics, geography
> 3. **Customer Profile** — Needs, buying behavior, willingness to pay
> 4. **Competitive Analysis** — Direct & indirect competitors, market share, SWOT

### 2.2.5 Marketing & Sales Strategy
- **4Ps Framework (McCarthy, 1960):** Product, Price, Place, Promotion
- Customer acquisition strategy (CAC — Customer Acquisition Cost)
- Distribution channels (online / offline / hybrid)
- Sales funnel and conversion metrics

### 2.2.6 Operations Plan
- Manufacturing / service delivery process
- Suppliers and raw materials
- Quality control procedures
- Technology stack and IT infrastructure
- Logistics and supply chain

### 2.2.7 Management Team
- Organizational hierarchy chart
- Key personnel with qualifications and experience
- Advisory board
- **Investor Rule:** A great team can save a mediocre idea; a poor team will kill a great idea

### 2.2.8 Financial Plan
This is the **analytical core** — covered in detail in Section 2.3 below.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Financial Formulas (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Financial Equations Used Inside a Business Plan:**

| # | Metric | Formula | Unit / Interpretation |
|---|--------|---------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Break-Even Point (Units)** | $\text{BEP}_{units} = \dfrac{F}{P - V}$ | Units to sell to cover costs |
| 2 | **Break-Even Point (Revenue)** | $\text{BEP}_{rev} = \dfrac{F}{1 - \dfrac{V}{P}}$ | Rupees of revenue at zero profit |
| 3 | **Return on Investment (ROI)** | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Net Profit}}{\text{Total Investment}} \times 100$ | Percentage return earned |
| 4 | **Payback Period** | $\text{PP} = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Net Cash Inflow}}$ | Years to recover investment |
| 5 | **Net Present Value** | $\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - I_0$ | Time-adjusted profitability |
| 6 | **Internal Rate of Return** | Equation where NPV = 0 | Discount rate that breaks even |
| 7 | **Gross Profit Margin** | $\text{GPM} = \dfrac{\text{Revenue} - \text{COGS}}{\text{Revenue}} \times 100$ | Pricing efficiency |
| 8 | **Debt-Equity Ratio** | $\text{D/E} = \dfrac{\text{Total Liabilities}}{\text{Shareholders' Equity}}$ | Financial leverage |
| 9 | **Customer Acquisition Cost** | $\text{CAC} = \dfrac{\text{Total Marketing Spend}}{\text{New Customers Acquired}}$ | Cost per new customer |
| 10 | **Customer Lifetime Value** | $\text{CLV} = \text{AOV} \times \text{Purchase Frequency} \times \text{Customer Lifespan}$ | Long-term customer worth |

> **Legend:** $F$ = Fixed Costs; $V$ = Variable Cost per unit; $P$ = Price per unit; $CF_t$ = Cash flow in year $t$; $r$ = Discount rate; $I_0$ = Initial investment; $AOV$ = Average Order Value.

---

## 2.4 Types of Business Plans

| Type | Length | Purpose | When Used |
|------|--------|---------|-----------|
| **Comprehensive / Traditional** | 25–40 pages | Bank loan, large investor pitch | Initial formal submission |
| **Mini-Plan** | 10–15 pages | Internal planning, quick iteration | Early-stage validation |
| **One-Page Plan** | 1 page | Quick pitch, executive overview | Networking, elevator pitch |
| **Growth / Strategic Plan** | 30+ pages | Existing firm expansion | Established companies |

## 2.5 Common Mistakes (KTU Examiner Emphasis)

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 7 Reasons Business Plans Fail Valuation:**
> 1. Unrealistic financial projections (no grounding in market data)
> 2. No clear definition of target customer
> 3. Weak competitive analysis
> 4. Ignoring risks and exit strategy
> 5. Plagiarized or template-based content (instant rejection)
> 6. Grammar and formatting errors (signals lack of professionalism)
> 7. Over-emphasis on product, under-emphasis on team and market

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Process — Business Plan Preparation Workflow

This section provides the **chronological, methodological steps** an entrepreneur follows to prepare a business plan. Every transition is explicitly justified.

---

## 3.1 The 8-Stage Preparation Process

### Step 1 — Idea Generation & Conceptualization

$$\text{Idea} \xrightarrow{\text{Feasibility Filter}} \text{Validated Concept}$$

The entrepreneur brainstorms potential business ideas using techniques such as **SCAMPER**, design thinking, or problem-solution fit analysis. Each idea is filtered against personal interest, market potential, and resource availability.

### Step 2 — Feasibility Study

$$\text{Feasibility} = f(\text{Market}, \text{Technical}, \text{Financial}, \text{Legal})$$

Four parallel studies must be completed:
- **Market Feasibility** — Is there demand?
- **Technical Feasibility** — Can we build it?
- **Financial Feasibility** — Can we afford it?
- **Legal Feasibility** — Are there regulatory barriers?

### Step 3 — Industry & Competitor Research

Data is gathered using:
- **Primary sources** — surveys, interviews, focus groups
- **Secondary sources** — IBISWorld, Statista, government reports, trade journals

The output is a **competitive matrix** mapping each competitor's strengths and weaknesses.

### Step 4 — Define Vision, Mission & Objectives

$$\text{Vision} \rightarrow \text{Mission} \rightarrow \text{Strategic Objectives} \rightarrow \text{Tactical Goals}$$

- **Vision** → Future aspiration (10–20 years)
- **Mission** → Current purpose statement
- **Objectives** → SMART goals (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound)

### Step 5 — Draft Each Section

Sections are drafted in the following logical order (NOT the final order in the document):

$$\text{Operations} \rightarrow \text{Products} \rightarrow \text{Market} \rightarrow \text{Marketing} \rightarrow \text{Team} \rightarrow \text{Financials} \rightarrow \text{Executive Summary}$$

The **Executive Summary is written last** because it summarizes all other sections.

### Step 6 — Financial Modelling

This is the most numerically rigorous step. The entrepreneur constructs:
- **Startup cost statement** (one-time expenses)
- **Projected Income Statement** (5 years)
- **Projected Balance Sheet**
- **Projected Cash Flow Statement**
- **Break-even analysis** (calculated using the formula in Section 2.3)

### Step 7 — Review, Edit & Validate

The draft is circulated to:
- A mentor or industry expert
- A chartered accountant (for financial validation)
- A lawyer (for legal compliance)
- A pilot reader who fits the target customer profile

### Step 8 — Final Compilation & Presentation

The document is formatted with:
- Professional cover page and table of contents
- Consistent fonts, headings, and numbering
- Visual aids (charts, graphs, infographics)
- Bound into a printed booklet or uploaded as a polished PDF

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Break-Even Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** An entrepreneur plans to sell handcrafted eco-friendly bags.
> - **Selling Price (P)** = ₹500 per bag
> - **Variable Cost per bag (V)** = ₹200
> - **Total Fixed Costs per month (F)** = ₹60,000

**Find the Break-Even Point in units and revenue.**

**Step 1 — Apply the BEP formula for units:**

$$\text{BEP}_{units} = \dfrac{F}{P - V}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$\text{BEP}_{units} = \dfrac{60{,}000}{500 - 200}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the denominator:**

$$500 - 200 = 300 \text{ rupees per bag}$$

**Step 4 — Final computation:**

$$\text{BEP}_{units} = \dfrac{60{,}000}{300} = 200 \text{ bags per month}$$

**Step 5 — Verify with revenue BEP:**

$$\text{BEP}_{rev} = 200 \text{ bags} \times ₹500 = ₹1{,}00{,}000 \text{ per month}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The entrepreneur must sell **at least 200 bags per month** to cover all fixed and variable costs. Beyond this point, every additional bag generates pure profit of ₹300 (contribution margin).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Section Length Recommendations

| Section | Recommended Length | Weightage in Valuation |
|---------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| Executive Summary | 1–2 pages | **30%** of reader attention |
| Company Description | 1–3 pages | 5% |
| Products/Services | 2–4 pages | 10% |
| Market Analysis | 4–6 pages | **25%** |
| Marketing Strategy | 3–5 pages | 10% |
| Operations Plan | 2–4 pages | 5% |
| Management Team | 2–3 pages | 10% |
| Financial Plan | 5–8 pages | **25%** |
| Appendices | As needed | 0% (supportive) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
