# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define MSME and list the revised classification criteria as per the 2020 amendment.
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** MSME stands for Micro, Small and Medium Enterprise, defined under the **MSMED Act, 2006**, as a business entity classified by investment in plant & machinery/equipment and annual turnover.
- **Revised Classification (2 Marks):**
  - **Micro:** Investment $\leq$ ₹1 Cr AND Turnover $\leq$ ₹5 Cr.
  - **Small:** Investment $\leq$ ₹10 Cr AND Turnover $\leq$ ₹50 Cr.
  - **Medium:** Investment $\leq$ ₹50 Cr AND Turnover $\leq$ ₹250 Cr.
  - The classification uses a **composite criterion** — both investment and turnover limits must be satisfied.

---

### Question 2: What is a Feasibility Study? Mention any two types.
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** A feasibility study is a systematic evaluation of a proposed business idea to determine its viability across market, technical, financial, and operational dimensions before significant capital commitment.
- **Types (2 Marks):**
  - **Market Feasibility:** Assesses demand, supply, competition, and customer profile.
  - **Technical Feasibility:** Evaluates production technology, raw material availability, and technical skills.
  - *(Any two valid types accepted; financial feasibility is also valid.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a): Explain in detail the various phases involved in starting an MSME unit. (7 Marks)
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The starting of an MSME unit is a structured entrepreneurial process comprising **ten sequential phases**:

**1. Idea Generation and Opportunity Identification (1 Mark):**
The entrepreneur identifies a market gap, a technological shift, or a personal passion. Tools like **brainstorming, SWOT, and customer discovery interviews** are used to screen and select a viable opportunity.

**2. Market and Technical Feasibility Study (1 Mark):**
The selected idea is tested for **market demand, technical viability, and preliminary financial soundness**. Outputs include a Feasibility Report covering market size, competition, technology availability, and rough cost estimates.

**3. Business Plan and Project Report Preparation (1 Mark):**
A comprehensive **Project Report** is prepared covering the promoter profile, product description, market analysis, operational plan, marketing strategy, and financial projections (P&L, Balance Sheet, Cash Flow). This report is the **primary document for bank loan applications**.

**4. Arrangement of Finance (1 Mark):**
Capital is arranged through a mix of **promoter's contribution (equity)** and **term loans (debt)** from banks, plus subsidies from government schemes like **PMEGP, MUDRA, Stand-Up India, and CLCSS**. The ideal debt-equity ratio is **2:1 to 3:1**.

**5. Statutory Registration and Licenses (1 Mark):**
The enterprise is legally registered through **Udyam Registration, PAN/TAN, GST, and industry-specific licenses** (Factory License, Trade License, Pollution Control Board NOC, FSSAI, BIS, etc.) as applicable.

**6. Location Selection and Plant Layout (0.5 Mark):**
The optimal site is selected based on **proximity to raw material, market, labor, and infrastructure**. The plant layout is designed — product layout, process layout, or hybrid.

**7. Procurement of Machinery and Equipment (0.5 Mark):**
Machines are procured from vendors (GeM, IndiaMART, OEMs), installed, and commissioned. Maintenance planning is set up.

**8. Manpower Recruitment and Training (0.5 Mark):**
The workforce is recruited and trained through campus placement, employment exchanges, and skill programs. **On-the-job training** and **safety training** are conducted.

**9. Trial Run and Pilot Production (0.5 Mark):**
A small batch is produced to test the production process, validate quality, refine operations, and confirm cost estimates.

**10. Commercial Production and Marketing (0.5 Mark):**
Full-scale production begins, and the product is launched in the market through **dealers, distributors, e-commerce, B2B/B2G channels**, supported by branding and promotional activities. A **feedback loop** ensures continuous improvement.

> **Conclusion:** These ten phases transform a raw business idea into a legally compliant, financially viable, and operationally active MSME, while minimizing risk and maximizing the chances of long-term survival.

---

#### Part (b): Discuss the role of a Project Report in starting an MSME. What are its key components? (7 Marks)
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Role of a Project Report (3.5 Marks):**
1. **Bank Loan Sanction:** Banks (SBI, Canara, Kerala Bank, KSFE) require a structured Project Report to evaluate loan eligibility, DSCR, and repayment capacity. Without it, term loan sanction is virtually impossible.
2. **Investment Attraction:** A well-prepared Project Report attracts **angel investors and VCs** for equity participation.
3. **Government Scheme Access:** Schemes like **PMEGP, CLCSS, and TUF** require the Project Report as a mandatory attachment.
4. **Internal Roadmap:** For the entrepreneur, the Project Report acts as a **blueprint** — defining the operational, marketing, and financial strategy for the first 3–5 years.
5. **Performance Benchmarking:** Actual performance is compared against projections, enabling course correction.

**Key Components of a Project Report (3.5 Marks):**
- **Promoter Profile:** Background, qualifications, experience.
- **Project Overview:** Product/service description, USPs, target market.
- **Market Analysis:** Industry overview, TAM-SAM-SOM, competition.
- **Location & Infrastructure:** Site details, plant layout, utilities.
- **Technical Plan:** Production process, machinery list, capacity.
- **Marketing Plan:** 4Ps (Product, Price, Place, Promotion).
- **Financial Plan:** Project cost, means of finance, P&L, Balance Sheet, Cash Flow, key ratios (IRR, DSCR, BEP).
- **SWOT & Risk Analysis:** Internal and external factors.
- **Implementation Schedule:** Phasing of activities.
- **Statutory & Environmental Compliance:** Required clearances.

> **Conclusion:** The Project Report is the **single most critical document** in the entire MSME setup process, serving as a financial, operational, and legal anchor for the venture.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### Part (a): Describe the various sources of finance available to an MSME entrepreneur in India. Highlight the role of government schemes. (7 Marks)
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Sources of Finance (5 Marks):**
1. **Personal Savings & Family Funds:** The promoter's own capital — usually the **equity component** of the project.
2. **Bank Loans:** Term loans and working capital from scheduled commercial banks (SBI, BOB, Kerala Bank), RRBs, and Cooperative Banks.
3. **Government Schemes:**
   - **PMEGP (Prime Minister's Employment Generation Programme):** Subsidy of **15%–35%** of project cost for SC/ST/Women/Minority/Ex-servicemen.
   - **MUDRA Loans:** Three tiers — **Shishu (≤₹50,000), Kishore (₹50,000–₹5 Lakh), Tarun (₹5–10 Lakh)**.
   - **Stand-Up India:** Loans of **₹10 Lakh–₹1 Crore** to SC/ST and Women entrepreneurs.
   - **CLCSS (Credit Linked Capital Subsidy Scheme):** 15% subsidy for technology upgradation.
   - **TUF (Technology Upgradation Fund):** For textile MSMEs.
4. **SIDBI (Small Industries Development Bank of India):** Direct and indirect financing to MSMEs.
5. **Angel Investors & Venture Capital:** For high-growth tech startups.
6. **Crowdfunding:** Platforms like Ketto, Indiegogo.
7. **Trade Credit:** Credit from suppliers for raw material procurement.

**Role of Government Schemes (2 Marks):**
- **Subsidy & Concessional Credit:** Reduce the effective cost of capital.
- **Collateral-Free Loans:** Address the collateral gap faced by first-generation entrepreneurs.
- **Capacity Building:** PMEGP beneficiaries receive mandatory EDP (Entrepreneurship Development Programme) training.
- **Social Inclusion:** Targeted schemes for SC/ST, Women, and Minority entrepreneurs ensure equitable growth.

> **Conclusion:** MSMEs benefit from a **diversified financing ecosystem** in India, with government schemes playing a critical role in **de-risking** and **democratizing** entrepreneurship.

---

#### Part (b): Explain the statutory registrations and licenses required to start an MSME unit in India. (7 Marks)
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**1. Udyam Registration (1 Mark):**
- Mandatory online registration at **udyamregistration.gov.in**.
- Single registration for all MSME benefits — replaces the older Udyog Aadhaar Memorandum.
- Provides **Udyam Certificate** with a unique **Udyam Registration Number (URN)**.

**2. Entity Registration (1 Mark):**
- Sole Proprietorship (simplest), Partnership Firm, LLP, or Private Limited Company.
- Registration with **Ministry of Corporate Affairs (MCA)** for companies/LLPs.

**3. PAN & TAN (0.5 Mark):**
- PAN from Income Tax Department for all entities.
- TAN for entities deducting TDS.

**4. GST Registration (1 Mark):**
- Mandatory if turnover exceeds **₹20 Lakhs** (₹10 Lakhs for special category states).
- Registration on **GSTN portal (gst.gov.in)**.

**5. Factory License (1 Mark):**
- Required under the **Factories Act, 1948** if the unit employs $\geq$ **10 workers with power** or $\geq$ **20 without power**.

**6. Trade License (0.5 Mark):**
- Issued by the **Local Municipality / Panchayat** for conducting business at a specific location.

**7. Pollution Control Board NOC (1 Mark):**
- Consent to Establish and Consent to Operate from **State Pollution Control Board** for manufacturing units.

**8. Industry-Specific Licenses (1 Mark):**
- **FSSAI License** (food MSMEs).
- **BIS Certification** (quality standards).
- **Drug License** (pharmaceutical MSMEs).
- **APMC License** (agricultural MSMEs).
- **IEC Code** (import/export MSMEs from DGFT).

> **Conclusion:** Statutory compliance is a **non-negotiable** phase of MSME setup. Skipping registrations not only attracts penalties but also disqualifies the unit from government schemes, bank loans, and tax benefits.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in "Phases of Starting an MSME" Questions:**
> 1. **Listing without explanation:** Writing only phase names (1 mark each) without 2–3 line explanations of *what is done* in each phase — loses 4–5 marks in a 14-mark question.
> 2. **Skipping Statutory Registration:** Many students omit Udyam Registration, GST, and industry-specific licenses. This is a **mandatory phase**.
> 3. **Ignoring Trial Run:** Trial run / pilot production is a *distinct phase* — students often merge it with commercial production and lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Confusing MSME Classification:** Mixing up the **2020 revised limits** (₹1 Cr/₹5 Cr/₹10 Cr/₹50 Cr/₹50 Cr/₹250 Cr) with the older pre-2020 SSI limits is a frequent error.
> 5. **No Conclusion in 14-Mark Answers:** KTU examiners award **0.5–1 mark for a meaningful conclusion** that summarizes the answer. Skipping it is a guaranteed deduction.
> 6. **Omitting Government Schemes in Finance:** For finance-related questions, students list only "bank loans" and forget **PMEGP, MUDRA, Stand-Up India** — losing 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):**
> - **MSME Classification (2020):** Micro $\leq$ ₹1 Cr / ₹5 Cr, Small $\leq$ ₹10 Cr / ₹50 Cr, Medium $\leq$ ₹50 Cr / ₹250 Cr.
> - **10 Phases:** Idea → Feasibility → Project Report → Finance → Registration → Location → Machinery → Manpower → Trial Run → Commercial Launch.
> - **Project Report is the most critical document** — used for bank loans, schemes, and internal planning.
> - **BEP** = Fixed Cost / (SP – VC) per unit; **DSCR** $\geq$ 1.5 required for term loan.
> - **Udyam Registration** is mandatory for all MSMEs (udyamregistration.gov.in).
> - **Government Schemes:** PMEGP (15–35% subsidy), MUDRA (Shishu/Kishore/Tarun), Stand-Up India (SC/ST/Women), CLCSS (15% tech upgradation), TUF (textiles).
> - **Composite Criterion:** Both investment AND turnover limits must be satisfied for classification.
> - **Statutory Licenses:** Factory License, Trade License, GST, PCB NOC, FSSAI, BIS — as applicable.
> - **Debt-Equity Ratio:** Ideal 2:1 to 3:1 for MSMEs.
> - **Trial Run ≠ Commercial Production:** Trial run is validation; commercial production is the actual revenue-generating phase.
> - **Most Asked Question Pattern:** "Explain the phases of starting an MSME unit" — answer with a structured 10-point list, brief explanation of each, and a concluding statement.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Training Requirements - Extension Training Institute
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT743 - ENTREPRENEURSHIP
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Training Requirements - Extension Training Institute

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Training Requirements** in the entrepreneurial context refer to the systematically identified competencies, skills, knowledge areas, and behavioural attributes that an aspiring or existing entrepreneur must acquire to successfully plan, launch, manage, and scale a business venture. These requirements are determined through a structured **Training Needs Assessment (TNA)** process that analyses the gap between the *current competency level* of the entrepreneur and the *desired competency level* demanded by the business environment.

**Extension Training Institute (ETI)** in the Indian entrepreneurship ecosystem refers to specialised institutional infrastructure, typically set up by the Government of India (Ministry of Skill Development and Entrepreneurship, formerly under the Ministry of MSME), that designs, develops, and delivers structured training, research, and consultancy services to first-generation entrepreneurs, micro-enterprise owners, and small business promoters. Prominent ETIs include the **Entrepreneurship Development Institute of India (EDI), Ahmedabad**, the **National Institute for Entrepreneurship and Small Business Development (NIESBUD), Noida**, and the **Indian Institute of Entrepreneurship (IIE), Guwahati**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** In the Entrepreneurship course, the term "Extension Training" specifically denotes *off-campus, outreach-oriented* training delivery models that extend institutional knowledge to grassroots-level entrepreneurs — distinct from formal classroom management education.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a young man named **Arjun** in rural Kerala who wants to start a small coconut-based products company. He has motivation and raw materials, but he does not know how to:
- prepare a project report,
- calculate break-even,
- file GST,
- market his product digitally, or
- maintain basic accounts.

Arjun has the **raw material (motivation)** but lacks the **machinery (skills)**. A **Training Requirement Analysis** is like a doctor diagnosing *which organs of his business body* are weak. Once diagnosed, an **Extension Training Institute** acts as a *mobile medical van* that travels to his village, sets up a *pop-up skill clinic*, trains him on exactly those weak areas, and follows up until he is "fit" to run the enterprise.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:**
> - *Training Requirements* = "What does the entrepreneur need to learn?"
> - *Extension Training Institute* = "Who delivers this learning at the grassroots level?"

## 1.3 Key Metrics & Benchmarks

The following standardised metrics are commonly used to evaluate the effectiveness of entrepreneurial training programmes delivered by ETIs:

- **Skill Coverage Index (SCI)** — measured as a percentage of intended competencies actually transferred.
- **Trainee-to-Trainer Ratio (TTR)** — typically maintained at **20:1** for effective skill-based delivery.
- **Post-Training Enterprise Survival Rate (PTESR)** — usually benchmarked at **≥ 60%** for a 2-year window.
- **Cost per Trainee (CPT)** — audited annually for subsidy efficiency.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Framework of Training Requirements (TR)

Training Requirements in entrepreneurship are not monolithic; they operate across **four interacting dimensions**. Understanding these dimensions is critical for answering KTU Part A and Part B questions.

### 2.1.1 The Four Pillars of Entrepreneurial Training Requirements

**(i) Functional / Technical Skills**
- Domain-specific knowledge — production technology, quality control, financial accounting, taxation (GST, income tax), statutory compliance.
- *Why it matters:* Without technical mastery, the product or service will fail on quality grounds.

**(ii) Managerial / Business Skills**
- Planning, organising, staffing, directing, controlling.
- Marketing mix, pricing strategy, distribution channel selection.
- *Why it matters:* Entrepreneurs wear the "manager" hat even in micro-enterprises.

**(iii) Entrepreneurial / Behavioural Skills**
- Risk-taking propensity, opportunity recognition, creativity, self-efficacy, locus of control, achievement motivation.
- *Why it matters:* Skills of *being* an entrepreneur, separate from skills of *running* a business.

**(iv) Personal / Life Skills**
- Communication, negotiation, time management, stress management, digital literacy.
- *Why it matters:* Determines long-term sustainability of the enterprise.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiners' Favourite Framework:** Memorise the four pillars as **F-M-E-P** (Functional, Managerial, Entrepreneurial, Personal). It is a high-frequency Part A and Part B question.

### 2.1.2 The Training Needs Assessment (TNA) Model

TNA follows a **three-gap analytical method**:

$$
\text{Training Gap} = \text{Desired Competency (DC)} - \text{Existing Competency (EC)}
$$

If Training Gap $\geq$ Threshold (typically $\geq 30\%$), training intervention is recommended.

The analytical steps are:

- **Step 1 — Organisational Analysis:** Identify business goals and translate them into competency requirements.
- **Step 2 — Task Analysis:** Decompose each business function into KSAOs (Knowledge, Skills, Abilities, Other characteristics).
- **Step 3 — Person Analysis:** Audit each entrepreneur's current KSAO level via interview, observation, or self-assessment.
- **Step 4 — Gap Statement:** Quantify the delta between required and existing competencies.
- **Step 5 — Training Intervention Design:** Select delivery mode (classroom, on-site, e-learning, mentoring, apprenticeship).

## 2.2 Extension Training Institutes — Structural Anatomy

### 2.2.1 Genesis & Mandate

Extension Training Institutes trace their origin to the **Industrial Policy Resolution of 1948** and the **Karve Committee Report (1955)**, which emphasised the need for institutional infrastructure to industrialise rural India. Three nodal ETIs were established by the Government of India:

| Institute | Location | Year Established | Apex Focus |
|---|---|---|---|
| **EDI** (Entrepreneurship Development Institute of India) | Ahmedabad, Gujarat | 1983 | Research, Training, Advocacy |
| **NIESBUD** (National Institute for Entrepreneurship and Small Business Development) | Noida, Uttar Pradesh | 1983 | National Coordinator, Curriculum |
| **IIE** (Indian Institute of Entrepreneurship) | Guwahati, Assam | 1993 | North-East India Outreach |

### 2.2.2 Core Functions of an Extension Training Institute

An ETI performs the **six core functions** enumerated below:

- **Curriculum Design:** Develops standardised entrepreneurship training modules.
- **Trainer Development:** Trains trainers (TOT — Training of Trainers) who cascade learning to districts.
- **Direct Training Delivery:** Conducts Entrepreneurship Development Programmes (EDPs).
- **Research & Publication:** Produces research on MSME dynamics, women entrepreneurship, startup ecosystems.
- **Consultancy:** Hand-holds District Industries Centres (DICs) and aspiring entrepreneurs.
- **Policy Advocacy:** Advises central and state governments on entrepreneurship policy design.

### 2.2.3 Typical Training Programmes Offered

- **EDP** — Entrepreneurship Development Programme (2–6 weeks).
- **WEDP** — Women Entrepreneurship Development Programme.
- **TEDP** — Technology Entrepreneurship Development Programme.
- **SEDS** — Skill-cum-Enterprise Development Scheme.
- **PMKVY** — Pradhan Mantri Kaushal Vikas Yojana (in partnership with NSDC).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Rule | Variable Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Training Gap | $\Delta T = DC - EC$ | $\Delta T \geq 0.3 \cdot DC$ triggers training |
| 2 | Coverage Ratio | $CR = \dfrac{N_{t}}{N_{p}} \times 100$ | $N_t$ = trained; $N_p$ = target population |
| 3 | Cost per Trainee | $CPT = \dfrac{T_{ce}}{N_{t}}$ | $T_{ce}$ = total expenditure |
| 4 | Survival Rate (2-yr) | $SR_{2y} = \dfrac{E_{a}}{E_{l}} \times 100$ | $E_a$ = active enterprises; $E_l$ = enterprises launched |
| 5 | TOT Multiplier | $M = 1 + (T_{tr} \times R_{c})$ | $T_{tr}$ = trainers trained; $R_c$ = cascade ratio (avg. 25) |
| 6 | Break-Even of Training | $BEP = \dfrac{F_{c}}{P_{f} - V_{c}}$ | $F_c$ = fixed cost; $P_f$ = per-trainee fee; $V_c$ = variable cost |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In all KTU 2024 answers, present numerical calculations with **unit consistency** and **two-decimal precision** unless otherwise specified.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

The Training Requirements–ETI ecosystem is operationally critical in:
- **Rural BPO & D2C startups** in Kerala's tier-3 towns.
- **MSME clusters** (e.g., Cochin Spice Trade, Kannur Handloom).
- **Women-led SHG-to-Enterprise conversions** under DAY-NULM.
- **Tech-startup incubators** integrated with ETI alumni mentorship networks.
- **Government flagship schemes** such as **Startup India**, **MUDRA**, and **SFURTI**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation — Training Gap Quantification

**Problem Statement:** A District Industries Centre (DIC) in Palakkad wishes to evaluate whether 200 aspiring women entrepreneurs require a formal Entrepreneurship Development Programme (EDP). A diagnostic survey reveals:

- Average *Desired Competency Score* on a 100-point scale: **85**
- Average *Existing Competency Score* on a 100-point scale: **48**
- The threshold for triggering training intervention: **30%** of Desired Competency.

**Step 1 — Compute the Absolute Training Gap.**

$$
\Delta T = DC - EC
$$

$$
\Delta T = 85 - 48 = 37 \text{ points}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Relative Training Gap (as a percentage of DC).**

$$
\Delta T_{\%} = \frac{\Delta T}{DC} \times 100
$$

$$
\Delta T_{\%} = \frac{37}{85} \times 100 = 43.53\%
$$

**Step 3 — Compare Against the Threshold.**

$$
\text{Decision} =
\begin{cases}
\text{Training Required,} & \text{if } \Delta T_{\%} \geq 30\% \\[4pt]
\text{Optional / On-job Coaching,} & \text{if } 15\% \leq \Delta T_{\%} < 30\% \\[4pt]
\text{No Training Required,} & \text{if } \Delta T_{\%} < 15\%
\end{cases}
$$

Since $\Delta T_{\%} = 43.53\% \geq 30\%$, **Training is mandated.**

**Step 4 — Compute the Total Training Load (Man-Weeks).**

$$
L_{T} = N_{t} \times H_{p} \times W_{d}
$$

Where $N_t$ = 200 trainees, $H_p$ = hours per programme = 120, $W_d$ = working days conversion factor = $\frac{1}{8}$.

$$
L_{T} = 200 \times 120 \times \frac{1}{8} = 3000 \text{ man-days}
$$

**Step 5 — Estimate Trainer Requirements.** Adopting KTU benchmark TTR = 20:1.

$$
N_{\text{trainers}} = \left\lceil \frac{N_{t}}{20} \right\rceil
$$

$$
N_{\text{trainers}} = \left\lceil \frac{200}{20} \right\rceil = 10 \text{ trainers}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right ETI Programme

The matrix below maps *identified training gaps* to *appropriate ETI programme design*. This is a high-yield answer structure for KTU Part B questions worth 7 marks.

| Training Gap Identified | Severity (Low / Mid / High) | Recommended ETI Programme | Delivery Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| No prior business experience | High | **EDP (Basic)** — 6 weeks | Residential, classroom |
| Existing MSE, wants to scale | Mid | **TEDP** (Technology) | Hybrid (online + on-site) |
| Women, first-generation | High | **WEDP** | Residential, mentoring |
| North-East geography | High | **IIE — North-East Outreach** | Mobile extension van |
| Train-the-Trainer cascade | High | **NIESBUD — TOT Programme** | Centralised at Noida |
| Policy or scheme awareness | Low–Mid | **Awareness Workshop** | One-day webinar |

## 3.3 Cost–Benefit Derivation for an ETI-Sponsored Programme

Assume an ETI invests **₹ 12,00,000** in a single EDP batch of **40 trainees**.

**Step 1 — Cost per Trainee.**

$$
CPT = \frac{T_{ce}}{N_{t}} = \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{40} = 30{,}000 \text{ ₹ / trainee}
$$

**Step 2 — Subsidy Recovery (Govt. grant covers 70%).**

$$
\text{Net Cost to Trainee} = CPT \times 0.30 = 30{,}000 \times 0.30 = 9{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 3 — 2-Year Survival Rate Audit.** If 26 of 40 enterprises are still active after 2 years:

$$
SR_{2y} = \frac{26}{40} \times 100 = 65\%
$$

Since $SR_{2y} = 65\% \geq 60\%$ benchmark, the programme is **declared effective** by NIESBUD evaluation norms.

**Step 4 — Social Return on Investment (SROI).**

$$
SROI = \frac{\text{PV of Outcomes}}{\text{PV of Inputs}} = \frac{40 \times 0.65 \times 4{,}50{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000}
$$

$$
SROI = \frac{1{,}17{,}00{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000} \approx 9.75
$$

An SROI of **9.75** means every ₹ 1 invested yields approximately ₹ 9.75 in long-term social-economic value — a strong justification for ETI programme continuation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Training Requirements to ETI Delivery Pipeline

```mermaid

    B1 --> B4[IIE Guwahati]

    B4 --> C1
    B5 --> C1
```
